data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL) After A 48% One-Year Rally?
data.description:
• For investors wondering whether Royal Caribbean Cruises at around US$281 a share is still offering value, or if the easy money has already been made, this article focuses squarely on what the current price might imply.
  • The stock has returned 5.2% over the last week, 3.3% over the last month and about 47.7% over the past year, while year to date it is roughly flat with a 0.6% decline.
  • Recent headlines have highlighted Royal Caribbean Cruises as one of the stronger movers in the travel and leisure space, with coverage often pointing to ongoing interest in cruise operators and changing risk perceptions across the sector. This backdrop helps explain why the share price has been active over shorter time frames even though the year to date result is close to unchanged.
  • Against that context, Royal Caribbean Cruises currently carries a valuation score of 5 out of 6, and the sections that follow break down how different valuation approaches line up on the stock, while keeping one more practical way to think about value for the end of the article.

Royal Caribbean Cruises delivered 47.7% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Hospitality industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Royal Caribbean Cruises Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value using a required rate of return. It is essentially an attempt to answer what all those future cash flows are worth in today’s dollars.

For Royal Caribbean Cruises, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow (FCF) is about $1.86b. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and extrapolated estimates point to FCF of $6.47b by 2030, with a full set of projections running from 2026 through 2035. Simply Wall St uses analyst inputs for the earlier years and then extends the trend for later years to build a longer term cash flow profile.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model indicates an estimated intrinsic value of about $337.96 per share. Against a recent share price around $281, the DCF outcome implies the stock is roughly 16.7% undervalued on this set of assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Royal Caribbean Cruises is undervalued by 16.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 57 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Royal Caribbean Cruises.

Approach 2: Royal Caribbean Cruises Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that each share currently generates. It helps you judge how much investors are willing to pay for today’s profits.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are perceived to be. Higher growth and lower risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower one.

Royal Caribbean Cruises currently trades on a P/E of 17.84x. That sits below the Hospitality industry average of about 21.77x and also below the broader peer group average of 36.97x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the stock is 28.64x, which is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be based on factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, risk characteristics, industry and market cap.

Compared with simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored because it blends these company specific inputs rather than relying on broad group averages. With the current P/E of 17.84x sitting below the Fair Ratio of 28.64x, this approach suggests that, on these measures, the shares may be trading at a lower valuation relative to their earnings.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Will meerecompany (KOSDAQ:049950) Spend Its Cash Wisely?
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although Amazon.com made losses for many years after listing, if you had bought and held the shares since 1999, you would have made a fortune. Nonetheless, only a fool would ignore the risk that a loss making company burns through its cash too quickly.

Given this risk, we thought we'd take a look at whether meerecompany (KOSDAQ:049950) shareholders should be worried about its cash burn. In this report, we will consider the company's annual negative free cash flow, henceforth referring to it as the 'cash burn'. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Does meerecompany Have A Long Cash Runway?

A company's cash runway is calculated by dividing its cash hoard by its cash burn. As at December 2025, meerecompany had cash of ₩26b and no debt. In the last year, its cash burn was ₩15b. That means it had a cash runway of around 22 months as of December 2025. While that cash runway isn't too concerning, sensible holders would be peering into the distance, and considering what happens if the company runs out of cash. Depicted below, you can see how its cash holdings have changed over time.

Check out our latest analysis for meerecompany

How Well Is meerecompany Growing?

Notably, meerecompany actually ramped up its cash burn very hard and fast in the last year, by 116%, signifying heavy investment in the business. While that's concerning on it's own, the fact that operating revenue was actually down 39% over the same period makes us positively tremulous. Considering these two factors together makes us nervous about the direction the company seems to be heading. In reality, this article only makes a short study of the company's growth data. This graph of historic earnings and revenue shows how meerecompany is building its business over time.

How Easily Can meerecompany Raise Cash?

meerecompany revenue is declining and its cash burn is increasing, so many may be considering its need to raise more cash in the future. Companies can raise capital through either debt or equity. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By comparing a company's annual cash burn to its total market capitalisation, we can estimate roughly how many shares it would have to issue in order to run the company for another year (at the same burn rate).

Since it has a market capitalisation of ₩119b, meerecompany's ₩15b in cash burn equates to about 12% of its market value. Given that situation, it's fair to say the company wouldn't have much trouble raising more cash for growth, but shareholders would be somewhat diluted.

So, Should We Worry About meerecompany's Cash Burn?

On this analysis of meerecompany's cash burn, we think its cash runway was reassuring, while its increasing cash burn has us a bit worried. We don't think its cash burn is particularly problematic, but after considering the range of factors in this article, we do think shareholders should be monitoring how it changes over time. data.title: 3 High Potential Canadian Junior Miners to Watch
data.description:
Global growth signals look patchy, inflation and energy costs keep flaring up, and government bond yields are pricing in extra geopolitical risk. In that setting, many investors are gravitating toward hard assets and strong balance sheets. One corner of the market that fits that brief is debt-free Canadian junior gold miners, which tie potential exploration upside to companies that are not carrying interest costs.

This article walks through three high potential companies and highlights why their cleaner capital structures may appeal to investors watching todayʼs macro and market crosscurrents.

  1. Advertisement

    Upside Gold (CNSX:UG)

Overview: Upside Gold is a Calgary-based exploration company advancing the Kena Gold-Copper Project in southeastern British Columbia. The project spans more than 10,000 hectares and hosts a historical gold resource of approximately 3.33 million ounces.

Market Cap : CA$59.8 million

Upside Gold provides exposure to a large-scale exploration asset in a Tier 1 jurisdiction. Hosting a substantial historical resource and supported by decades of exploration data, the project offers a foundation from which the company aims to expand and upgrade mineralization. The presence of both gold and copper also introduces potential by-product credits, which could enhance project economics if future studies confirm their commercial viability.

Looking ahead, the company’s planned 2026 drilling campaign represents a key milestone. Exploration results that demonstrate continuity of mineralization or expand the existing resource base could materially influence investor perception. Conversely, delays or inconclusive results would highlight the inherent uncertainty of the exploration stage. Investors seeking additional context can refer to our recent article on Upside Gold’s updated resource estimate .

A Simply Wall St community narrative estimates a fair value of ~CA$5.07 per share , based on peer comparisons using an EV-to-resource-ounce methodology. This valuation assumes successful resource expansion and continued exploration progress, though these outcomes remain subject to execution risk.

Learn more about Upside Gold’s Kena Project by watching this YouTube interview with their CEO, Sophy Cesar.

2. Eskay Mining (TSXV:ESK)

Overview: Eskay Mining is a Toronto based explorer focused on the ESKAY Corey property in northwestern British Columbia, targeting gold silver volcanogenic massive sulphide deposits along with nickel, copper and platinum group elements in gabbro hosted systems.

Market Cap : CA$80.2 million

Eskay Mining sits at the higher risk, higher potential end of this debt free junior gold space, combining a 100% owned land package in the Golden Triangle with an experienced management team and board that average more than a decade in their roles. The 2026 exploration plan leans into AI assisted analysis, new porphyry and vein gold targets and follow up drilling on earlier Vermillion TM work.

This may appeal if you want exposure to active discovery work rather than production. Set against that, the company has no meaningful revenue, ongoing losses, a high P/B multiple and relies on external funding. All of these factors leave little margin for disappointment and make Eskay a name where position sizing and risk tolerance matter.

Eskayʼs AI driven exploration, concentrated Golden Triangle focus and debt - free balance sheet can look like an overlooked high risk, high potential setup. We walk through more detail in ESK’s company report .

TSXV:ESK P/B Ratio as at Apr 2026

3. Canterra Minerals (TSXV:CTM)

Overview : Canterra Minerals is a Vancouver based explorer focused on acquiring and advancing early stage mineral projects across Newfoundland, Alberta and Ontario, targeting zinc, lead, copper, silver, gold, diamonds and volcanogenic massive sulphide deposits.

Market Cap : CA$68.2 million

Canterra sits at the more speculative end of this list. Recent drill results at the Buchans project, including thick CuEq intercepts and high grade sub intervals, show why some investors are watching closely despite five years of roughly 9.6% annual earnings declines and ongoing losses.

A fully funded 2026 drilling program of up to 15,000 metres is set to take place across Buchans, the Victoria Lake Supergroup portfolio, and Wilding Gold. Combined with recent high-grade gold discoveries at Wilding, this creates a busy pipeline of potential catalysts. However, the company currently generates little to no revenue and remains reliant on external funding.

Canterraʼs thick CuEq hits and fully funded 2026 drill program could be masking a turning point in this higher risk explorer. The company report for Canterra Minerals highlights how these factors could reshape expectations.

Bannerman Energy Ltd engages in the exploration and development of uranium properties in Africa. The AU$843m market-cap company posted a loss in its most recent financial year of AU$4.1m and a latest trailing-twelve-month loss of AU$2.2m shrinking the gap between loss and breakeven. Consensus from 4 of the Australian Oil and Gas analysts is that Bannerman Energy is on the verge of breakeven.
